questions.question: Define a database transaction and explain its importance in DBMS.
keyPoints: A transaction is a logical unit of work consisting of multiple database operations executed as a single unit
Ensures data integrity when multiple related operations must succeed or fail together
Examples: banking transfer, airline booking, e-commerce order processing
Provides reliability and consistency in database operations

questions.question: Explain the four ACID properties of transactions with one example for each.
keyPoints: Atomicity: All-or-nothing execution (e.g., money transfer either completes fully or not at all)
Consistency: Database transforms from one valid state to another (e.g., account balance never goes negative)
Isolation: Concurrent transactions appear independent (e.g., two people booking different seats)
Durability: Committed changes are permanent (e.g., saved transaction survives system crash)

questions.question: List and briefly describe the five states of a transaction.
keyPoints: Active: Transaction is currently executing
Partially Committed: Final operation has been executed, waiting for confirmation
Committed: Transaction has successfully completed and changes are saved
Aborted: Transaction failed and has been rolled back
Terminated: Transaction has ended (either committed or aborted)

questions.question: What is a serial schedule? Why is it considered consistent but not efficient?
keyPoints: Serial schedule: Transactions execute one after another with no interleaving of operations
Consistent because no interference between transactions - no lost updates, dirty reads, or inconsistent analysis
Not efficient because it doesn't utilize system resources optimally - long transactions block shorter ones
Reduces throughput and increases waiting time

questions.question: Differentiate between COMMIT and ROLLBACK operations in SQL.
keyPoints: COMMIT: Permanently saves all changes made during the transaction to the database
ROLLBACK: Undoes all changes and restores database to state before transaction began
COMMIT makes changes visible to other transactions; ROLLBACK ensures no changes are visible
Used when: COMMIT on success, ROLLBACK on error or to undo partial work

questions.question: What are the three main concurrency problems? Explain each briefly.
keyPoints: Lost Update Problem: One transaction's update is overwritten by another transaction's update
Uncommitted Dependency (Dirty Read): Reading data written by an uncommitted transaction
Inconsistent Analysis (Non-Repeatable Read): Reading inconsistent values when another transaction updates data

questions.question: Define a recoverable schedule and a cascadeless schedule.
keyPoints: Recoverable Schedule: A transaction never reads data written by an uncommitted transaction; if T2 reads T1's uncommitted data, T2 must commit after T1
Cascadeless Schedule (Strict): No transaction reads or writes data written by an uncommitted transaction; prevents cascading rollbacks
Cascadeless is a stricter form of recoverable schedule

questions.question: Explain the Two-Phase Commit (2PC) protocol used in distributed databases. Discuss its phases and how it ensures atomicity.
keyPoints: 2PC ensures atomic commitment across multiple databases/nodes
Phase 1 (Prepare): Coordinator asks all participants if they can commit; participants lock resources and write to log, respond with 'prepared' or 'abort'
Phase 2 (Commit): If all vote 'prepared', coordinator sends 'commit'; participants commit and release locks; if any votes 'abort', coordinator sends 'rollback'
Ensures atomicity: either all participants commit or all abort
Limitations: Blocking problem if coordinator fails, synchronous operations

questions.question: Consider two transactions T1 and T2 as follows: T1: R(X), W(X), R(Y), W(Y); T2: R(X), W(X). Show with schedules why serial schedules are always consistent and discuss how concurrency control achieves consistency in non-serial schedules.
keyPoints: Serial schedule T1→T2: T1 reads/writes X and Y, then T2 reads/writes X - no interference, always consistent
Serial schedule T2→T1: T2 reads/writes X first, then T1 reads/writes X and Y - also consistent
Interleaved schedule problems: Lost update (both write X), Dirty read (T2 reads T1's uncommitted X), Inconsistent analysis (T1 reads X twice while T2 modifies it)
Concurrency control mechanisms (locking, timestamp ordering, optimistic concurrency) prevent these problems
Goal: Allow efficient interleaving while maintaining consistency equivalent to some serial schedule

questions.question: What is a deadlock? Discuss strategies for deadlock detection and prevention in database systems.
keyPoints: Deadlock: Circular wait condition where two or more transactions wait indefinitely for each other to release locks
Detection: Wait-for graph (cycles indicate deadlock), timeout-based detection
Prevention: No circular wait - impose ordering on lock acquisition; Wait-Die and Wound-Wait schemes
Resolution: Choose a victim transaction, rollback it, release locks; victim selection based on cost
Prevention techniques: Lock ordering (always acquire locks in same order), lock timeout, immediate validation

questions.question: Discuss the concept of transaction isolation levels in SQL. How do different isolation levels address concurrency problems?
keyPoints: READ UNCOMMITTED: Allows dirty reads, lowest isolation, addresses no concurrency problems
READ COMMITTED: Only reads committed data, prevents dirty reads but not non-repeatable reads
REPEATABLE READ: Prevents dirty and non-repeatable reads but may allow phantom reads
SERIALIZABLE: Highest isolation, ensures serializable schedule, prevents all concurrency problems
Higher isolation = more locking = reduced concurrency but better consistency
